role_hints.role: Operations engineers and administrators (the delivery core)
role_hints.oews_basis: 15-1252 Software Developers 23.9% at 4-digit 5415 — for this code much of the technical wage bill is really systems/network administrators (15-1244) and operations staff in the unlisted tail; est. ~30-35% combined
role_hints.note: Monitoring, patching, incident triage, capacity reporting and runbook execution are the exact target of AIOps/self-healing tooling — high within-role fraction; physical data-center hands (rack/stack, media handling) and client-site presence persist.

role_hints.oews_basis: 15-1232 Computer User Support Specialists 3.3% at 4-digit; likely higher in facilities-management contracts, est. ~6-10%
role_hints.note: Tier-1/2 support under outsourcing contracts — directly AI-automatable; contracts often price it per ticket or per seat, so automation economics hinge on contract form.
Service-level reporting, transition planning docs and audits automate; client governance interaction persists.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-3021 Computer & Information Systems Managers 8.3% + 13-1082 Project Management Specialists 3.0%
role_hints.note: SLA reporting and coordination automate; the contract-governance relationship with client IT leadership persists and is the retention asset.
Long-cycle outsourcing-contract sales and renewals persist; internal ops automate.

role_hints.oews_basis: remainder beyond the listed top-10 (top-10 sums to ~61% at 4-digit), est. ~25-30% — includes shift operators and physical facilities staff
role_hints.note: Back office automates; shift operations staff are the AIOps target; physical facilities work is not automatable.

source_hints.source: ISG (Information Services Group) Index and managed-services market reports
source_hints.why: The standard tracker of IT outsourcing/managed-services contract activity and pricing trends — evidence for both adoption of automation in delivery and market direction.

source_hints.source: HFS Research / Everest Group coverage of AIOps and IT operations outsourcing
source_hints.why: Analyst coverage quantifying automation penetration in outsourced IT operations delivery.

source_hints.source: Public outsourcing-provider disclosures (Kyndryl, DXC earnings commentary)
source_hints.why: The large pure-plays document exactly how automation savings interact with outsourcing contract pricing (productivity commitments, contract-value erosion) — the capture mechanism at scale.

source_hints.source: Solganick / Canalys IT-services M&A coverage (managed infrastructure segment)
source_hints.why: Deal activity in managed-infrastructure/data-center services for B and M; distinguish from MSP-SMB deals in 541512.

source_hints.source: No dedicated owner-demographic source known for this code
source_hints.why: Small-firm owner data is absent; the code skews to larger firms anyway (151 firms at 500+) — flag and rely on ABS proxies if needed.

capture_questions: Facilities-management/outsourcing contracts price as multi-year fixed fees, per-device/per-seat rates, or cost-plus with committed year-over-year productivity glidepaths — under these structures, automation savings are typically PRE-COMMITTED to the client at renewal (the Kyndryl/DXC pattern of shrinking contract value). In the $1-10M EBITDA tier, what contract forms dominate, and is there any evidence operators retain automation gains past one renewal cycle?

Who owns the client relationship — the firm holds multi-year contracts with real switching costs (transition risk keeps clients captive), but renewals are procurement-run and benchmarked; does incumbency pricing power survive a benchmarking clause?

Cloud migration is the structural bypass: clients don't need generic AI to leave — they need AWS/Azure, which has been shrinking the on-prem facilities-management base for a decade; what is the evidenced trajectory of the addressable base, and does AI accelerate workload migration off managed on-prem estates?

For the surviving base (regulated, legacy, sovereignty-bound workloads), does compliance/audit accountability create durable fees that AI operations tooling cannot displace — and how big is that residue?
terminal_value_question: This code sells the ongoing human operation of client IT estates — precisely the function that both cloud (structurally, for a decade) and AIOps (now) replace. The mechanism to check is contractual: outsourcing renewals with benchmarking clauses and productivity commitments hand automation gains to clients on a fixed cycle, while the addressable estate itself migrates to hyperscalers whose operations are already automated. Is there a defensible terminal niche (regulated/legacy/sovereign workloads with audit-accountability fees), or is this a melting-ice-cube base where AI is not the operator's tool but the client's exit ramp? Score the niche, not the 2022 snapshot.
special_notes: Labor share 0.3989 is the group's lowest for a reason: receipts carry pass-through hosting, hardware, software licenses and subcontracted capacity — normalize before inferring margin structure, and expect wide variance between labor-based contracts and pass-through-heavy ones.

Sibling boundaries: 541513 = ongoing management/operation of a CLIENT'S computer systems and facilities (outsourced IT ops, data-center operation for the client); 541512 = designing/integrating systems and the SMB MSP model; 541511 = custom programming; 541519 = residual incl. disaster recovery. Also outside the family: 518210 (computing infrastructure providers/hosting — the provider owns the infrastructure) contaminates market data constantly; if the firm owns the data center, it is not 541513.

Size-structure warning: this code is big-firm skewed (151 firms at 500+, avg $117M revenue) — the fragmented-small-firm roll-up pattern fits poorly; n_band 583 exists but verify how many are independent firms vs. subsidiaries/captives before scoring B.

Secular headwind predates AI: cloud migration has been shrinking traditional facilities management since the 2010s — single-vintage (2022) receipts overstate the durable base; check the trend, and do not attribute cloud-driven decline to AI or vice versa.

Contract-form deflation is the central capture fact: benchmarking clauses and productivity glidepaths in outsourcing contracts are explicit, documented mechanisms that pass efficiency gains to clients — model pi_dist off contract structure, not off generic services intuition.
